title: Chapter 28: The Aftermath
content: Three weeks.
Three weeks of one world, one timeline, one life, and I still reach for the app.

It happens at random — standing at the counter between orders, walking home along the harborside, lying in bed at 2 AM with the ceiling pressing down and my thumb swiping through screens looking for the little fork icon that used to sit between Spotify and my banking app. My finger finds the spot where it lived, and there's nothing. Just the smooth glass, just the grid of normal apps that normal people use to do normal things.
No Fork. No paths. No escape hatch.
I'm free. And it's terrifying.

You'd think freedom would feel like flying. You'd think shedding three timelines' worth of impossible choices would feel like stepping out of a corset, like breathing deep for the first time. And it does — sometimes. In flashes. When I'm steaming milk and the hiss of the wand is the loudest thing in the room, or when Gatsby climbs into my lap and purrs so hard his whole body vibrates against my thighs. Those moments are light. Those moments are mine.

But then there are the other moments. The ones where the walls of one life feel too small, too singular, too fixed. When my brain starts calculating alternate routes the way it used to — scanning for the shimmer, reaching for the seam between worlds — and comes up empty. When I realize that this is it. This room, this town, this coffee-stained apron, this bed. Every morning I will wake up and there will be only one day ahead of me. Only one version of it.
No take-backs. No reloads. No forking.
Just this.
The café is thriving.

Not in the way that makes the business section — there's no tech boom narrative here, no viral moment, no aesthetic instagrammability that draws influencers in clusters. It's thriving the way a garden thrives: slowly, rootedly, with the kind of growth you only notice when you step back and realize the tomatoes have taken over the whole fence.

With Julian no longer hiding — from the café, from the town, from himself — things have opened up. Weekend brunch, for starters. Eggs Benedict with proper hollandaise that he makes standing at the stove in a flour-dusted apron, swearing softly at the whisk. A small bookshelf corner near the window, curated by Mrs. O'Connell from the bookshop next door — she brings paperbacks in milk crates and takes her payment in flat whites. Live music on Fridays: local musicians, fiddles and acoustic guitars, the old guys from the pub who play sea shanties like they're holding the coast together with chord progressions.
Not Julian, though. When I asked, he shook his head. "My guitar days are behind me."

But I catch him humming. Behind the counter, folding napkins, walking home with his hands in his pockets — low, unconscious melodies that trail off when he notices he's doing it. Like a language he still speaks but doesn't know he remembers.

I make lattes. I mop floors. I feed cats — Gatsby and the two tabbies who've adopted the café's back door as their personal restaurant. I restock the biscotti jars and wipe down tables and pretend to understand the Irish politics Mrs. O'Connell argues about with the morning regulars. And for the first time since graduation — since the apartment in Chicago, since the résumé I never finished, since the life plan I abandoned in a Google Doc somewhere — I am not thinking about the life I'm supposed to be living.
I'm living this one.
And then.
It hits me in the supply closet.

Which is such an undignified place for grief to find you — between the industrial-sized bottle of floor cleaner and a torn bag of cat food that leaks kibble dust onto the concrete floor. But that's where I am when the wave crests.

I'd been fine. Genuinely, honestly fine. I was restocking paper cups, and I cracked open a new sleeve, and the cardboard had that fresh-pressed smell — warm, woody, a little sweet — and underneath it, threaded through like a crime scene fiber, cedar and sandalwood.
Julian A's cologne.

Except there is no Julian A. Not in this world. Not anymore. He existed — that suit, that skyline, that voice saying Reste in a gallery full of gold-leaf paintings — and he existed so vividly that my body remembers him before my mind can catch up. My lungs seize. My eyes burn. I sit down hard on the floor, back against the shelving unit, and I breathe.
In through the nose. Out through the mouth. The way the therapist I never went to would have told me to.

It's not always cedar and sandalwood. Sometimes it's Sancerre — I'll drink a glass of water and taste wine on the back of my tongue, cold and mineral and bright, and I'll think of Paris, of Julian B pouring with both hands and telling me about the woman who loved butter, and the grief will buckle me. Sometimes it's a phrase in French I almost understand, or the quality of light through a window at dusk that matches a evening I lived in a city that doesn't exist anymore, in a timeline that collapsed into this one like a star into a black hole.

I mourn two men. Two men who are also one man. One man who is also sitting right outside this closet, making a customer laugh about the weather, his voice warm and easy and present in a way the others never get to be anymore.
The grief is real.
The love was real.
The timelines were real, even if they're gone now.
Julian finds me once.

I don't hear him coming — I'm too busy trying to breathe through the tightening in my chest, eyes squeezed shut, fingers pressing into my sternum like I can physically hold myself together. The door opens, and there's a beat of silence. Then he doesn't ask what's wrong.
He sits down.

Right there on the floor, between the cleaning supplies and the cat food, his long legs folding up like he does this every day. He doesn't hover. He doesn't reach for me with that urgent, let-me-fix-it energy that makes me want to crawl out of my skin. He just — settles. Shoulder against the shelving unit, hip near mine but not touching, and after a moment, his hand finds mine. Fingers threading through fingers. The squeeze is gentle. Steady. A question and an answer at once.
I open my eyes. He's looking at me, and his face is open in that way his face gets when he's really seeing someone — not performing attentiveness, not waiting for his turn to speak. Just present.
"You get this look sometimes," he says. Quiet. No judgment. "Like you're watching something I can't see."
I could lie. The words are right there: I'm fine, just tired, long day. I've said them a thousand times. They fit in the mouth like mints, easy and small and meaningless.
I choose truth.
"I'm mourning," I say, and my voice cracks on the word like it's something I've been holding in my jaw and finally let drop. "People I lost. Places that don't exist anymore."

He absorbs this. I watch it move through him — not a flinch, not a flinch at all, but a slow recognition, like watching weather cross a landscape. He doesn't ask who, or where, or how. He just takes it in, and then:
"Do you want to talk about them?"
I shake my head. The lump in my throat would swallow any words I tried. "Not yet."
He squeezes my hand. Once. "I'll be here when you're ready."

No pressure. No timeline. No let's-schedule-a-check-in, no I'm-here-if-you-need-me delivered with that particular tone that actually means please don't need me. Just presence. Just his warm hand in mine and the hum of the espresso machine through the wall and the smell of coffee and cat food and the solid fact of him sitting on the floor of a supply closet because that's where I happened to be.
This is why I chose this world.
Mika texts on a Thursday.
It's been two days since the supply closet, and I'm behind the counter, wiping down the steam wand, when my phone buzzes.
**Mika:** How's the one-life thing?

I stare at it for a moment. The one-life thing. She makes it sound like a wellness retreat, like something you can review on Yelp. Three stars, would recommend, very grounding, the existential dread really exfoliates.

But she's asking because she knows — not the details, not the specifics, but enough. Enough to know that I'm doing something harder than I've ever done, which is living in the only body and the only day and the only story I get.
I take a photo.

The café at sunset — I've gotten good at this, at catching the light when it goes amber and slants through the windows and turns everything gold-edged and painterly. Gatsby is asleep on the counter, one paw draped over the espresso machine's drip tray like he owns the place. And in the window, silhouetted against the last of the light, Julian. A book open in his lap, his head bent, the shape of him dark against the glowing sky.
I send it without a caption.
Her reply comes fast.
**Mika:** You look happy.
A beat. Then:
**Mika:** Not performing happy. Actually happy.
I read it three times. Then I set my phone down on the counter, and I press both palms flat against the stainless steel, and I let it land. Not performing happy. Actually happy.

It's the most validating text I've ever received. Because Mika has known me since orientation week, since the dining hall and the bad decisions and the late-night conversations about who we were going to become. She's seen every version of my smile — the polite one, the defensive one, the one I wore like armor when I didn't know how to be soft. And she looked at that photo and saw through to the thing underneath.
Actually happy.

Not because everything is perfect. Not because the grief is gone. But because underneath the grief, underneath the waves that still knock me sideways sometimes, there's something solid. Something that holds.
I am actually happy. I didn't know that was possible.
That night, I tell him everything.

We're upstairs in his apartment — the small rooms above the café, cozy and cluttered with the artifacts of a life being lived rather than curated. Gatsby is grooming himself on the windowsill. The harbor lights blink through the glass. Julian is on the couch with his book, and I'm at the kitchen table with a cold cup of tea I stopped drinking an hour ago, and the words have been building in me like water behind a dam, and I can't hold them anymore.
"Julian."
He looks up. And something in my voice must tell him this is different, because he sets the book down without marking the page, and he gives me his full attention, and he waits.
I tell him about the app.

I tell him about the Fork — how it appeared on my phone the night of my graduation, how it offered me three paths, three lives, three versions of everything. I tell him about the three timelines, the three worlds, the three Julians. I tell him about Julian A in his bespoke suits and his penthouse and his skyline and the way he said Reste like it was both a command and a prayer. I tell him about Julian B in his Paris kitchen, golden and warm, cooking me dinner with wine-stained hands and kissing me like I was something worth savoring. I tell him about the Garden of Forking Paths, the merge, the choice — the impossible, world-breaking choice I had to make when the walls between timelines started collapsing.
I tell him I chose this world. This life. Him.

Not because the others weren't beautiful. They were. They were so beautiful it still breaks me to remember them. But because this — the café, the cats, the floor of the supply closet, his hand in mine — this is where I was most myself.
He listens.

He doesn't interrupt. Doesn't flinch at the impossible parts — the reality-shifting, the app, the three concurrent lives. His face moves through expressions I can't fully name, and his hands stay open on his knees, and he listens like he's hearing a language he doesn't speak but can feel the meaning of, like music without lyrics.

When I finish, the apartment is quiet. Gatsby has stopped grooming. The harbor lights keep blinking. And Julian is quiet for a long time — long enough that I feel the silence start to congeal, start to solidify into something I might not be able to breathe through, and I'm about to say something, anything, take it back or soften it or —
"So somewhere," he says, and his voice is calm, almost wondering, "there was a version of me in a suit making you cry?"
A laugh escapes me. Bursting out of my chest like a bird taking flight, sudden and wet and so unexpected I almost don't recognize the sound. "Yes."
"And a version of me cooking you dinner in Paris?"
"Also yes."
He shakes his head. A smile ghosts across his mouth, there and gone. "Sounds like I missed out."
And then — more seriously. His eyes finding mine, steady and warm and so present it aches.

"They were me. I believe that." He says it like he's turning the idea over, testing its weight. "And I'm sorry they're gone." A breath. "But I'm going to spend my life being enough that you don't regret it."
He doesn't say it like a promise.
He says it like a fact. Like the sunrise. Like gravity.

I sit with it — the weight of those words settling over me like a blanket, like the first warm thing after a long cold. The truth is out now, all of it, and he's still here. He didn't run. He didn't flinch. He looked at the impossible, impossible shape of my life, and he found the part that mattered — that I chose him — and he built something around it. Not a cage. A home.

I cross the room. I sit beside him on the couch, and I lean into him, and his arm comes around me like it's the most natural thing in the world, and I feel the solid warmth of his chest and the scratch of his sweater and the steady drum of his heartbeat against my ear.
He's going to be enough.

And for the first time — standing in the ruins of three timelines with nothing but one life and one man and one small Irish town and one orange cat who has relocated from the windowsill to my lap — I believe it.
